sections.about: This metric captures total recordable work-related injuries for employees. Recordable work-related injury or ill-health includes death, days away from work, restricted work/job transfer, medical treatment beyond first aid, or loss of consciousness. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata improved from 390 in FY23 to 208 in FY24 and 196 in FY25, but remained materially above JSW, SAIL and Jindal. Jindal led consistently, moving from 3 to 3 to 0. JSW improved to 8 in FY24 before worsening to 21 in FY25; SAIL moved from 28 to 33 to 29. Tata’s higher count may partly reflect scale, broader reporting boundary and mature capture of recordable incidents, but the peer gap is too large to treat only as reporting maturity. Tata’s own FY25 disclosure confirms 196 employee recordable injuries and its reliance on incident investigation, EnsafeNxt and 100% assessment of health and safety practices.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranked 4th at 196 while Jindal reported 0. Percentage gap is not meaningful because the best value is zero; the absolute gap is 196 recordable employee injuries. Tata’s rank stayed 4th across all three years despite reductions, showing that absolute performance improved but competitive positioning did not.
sections.targets: Tata states zero harm as the safety ambition. Jindal discloses Zero Harm and continuous improvement in safety indicators.
